channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: U.S. and Iran Trade Strikes, Further Threatening Negotiations
item.description: The U.S. military said Iran had launched a ballistic missile toward Kuwait. Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said it had targeted an unspecified U.S. base in retaliation for earlier strikes.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Majid Saeedi/Getty Images

item.title: The Mideast Is Baffled by Trump’s Call to Expand Abraham Accords
item.description: The president said more countries should be required to recognize Israel as part of a deal to end the war with Iran. Analysts say the chances of that happening are close to zero.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Isabel Kershner and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with leaders from Israel, Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates at the signing of the Abraham Accords in Washington in 2020.

item.title: Justice Dept. Is Said to Open Criminal Inquiry of E. Jean Carroll, Who Accused Trump of Rape
item.description: Ms. Carroll, who prevailed in a civil trial after accusing President Trump of sexual abuse, is the latest target in a Justice Department campaign going after his perceived enemies.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll leaving court in Manhattan in 2024. Donald J. Trump later lost an appeal of a $5 million verdict against him in the case.

item.title: These Are Some of the Trump Justice Dept.’s Targets
item.description: When he returned to office, President Trump vowed revenge against those he said had challenged or defied him. Here’s an incomplete list of the people his Justice Department has targeted.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Justice in Washington, this year.

item.title: ‘Both Parties Kind of Get It Wrong’: The Young Men Who May Swing the Midterms
item.description: Many Gen Z men who voted for Donald Trump are dismayed by his time in office. But they say they are not hearing an appealing pitch from Democrats, either.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times

item.title: James Talarico Opens Texas Senate Campaign Against Ken Paxton With Houston Rally
item.description: Personal attacks and a packed campaign rally set the tone for what is likely to be a rancorous contest in Texas for U.S. Senate between James Talarico and Ken Paxton.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: State Representative James Talarico campaigned in Houston on Wednesday.

item.title: Judge Declines, for Now, to Block Mail-In Voting Changes Ordered by Trump
item.description: The ruling allowed the Trump administration to continue to pursue changes that include enlisting the Postal Service in checking voters against a national database.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A voter casting a mail-in ballot in June.

item.title: Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer Will Not Run for President in 2028
item.description: Ms. Whitmer had been viewed as part of a field of possible Democratic candidates. She has been governor for two terms, and is barred from running again.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer of Michigan at an event in Detroit in April.

item.title: The Nonfiction Everyone Will Be Reading This Summer
item.description: Memoirs, histories, true crime, investigations and much more.
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: The Novels Everyone Will Be Reading This Summer
item.description: New fiction from Maggie O’Farrell, Ann Patchett, Colson Whitehead, Silvia Moreno-Garcia and much more.
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Bari Weiss Names Nick Bilton to Lead CBS’s ’60 Minutes’
item.description: Mr. Bilton, a tech journalist and filmmaker, said he would bring new voices to the show. The overhaul by Bari Weiss, CBS’s editor in chief, included the firing of the correspondent Cecilia Vega.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Matt Winkelmeyer/Getty Images
item.media:description: Nick Bilton is a filmmaker and former New York Times columnist.

item.title: Guatemala Agrees to Joint Drug Gang Strikes With U.S., Expanding Trump’s Campaign
item.description: The deal is part of a broader effort by the Trump administration to press Latin American countries to agree to joint operations inside their borders.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Daniele Volpe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Security forces outside a prison in Guatemala City after a wave of riots and gang violence in January.

item.title: Cuba Loses Its Chance at Fuel After Russian Tanker Changes Route
item.description: A Russian tanker seemingly en route to Cuba with a lifeline of fuel has changed direction, a painful development for the island suffering under a U.S. oil blockade.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Jorge Luis Banos/Associated Press
item.media:description: Yankiel Perez carried buckets of water on Monday outside a building in Havana.

item.title: I Tried to Sell My House With A.I.
item.description: Over five frantic days, I gambled my family’s life savings on a hunch that A.I. could outperform a real estate agent.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson

item.title: ‘Vibrant as Ever’: Harvard’s Jewish Life Thrives as Trump Investigates
item.description: Trump officials have focused on episodes during protests over the war in Gaza at Harvard and other schools. Jews on campus describe a changed atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: At Harvard University, students and others say the culture is different from the one depicted in the Trump administration’s lawsuit.

item.title: In Argentina, U.S. Tech Billionaire Peter Thiel Finds An Escape
item.description: The billionaire’s new roots in Argentina are said to be partly motivated by concerns about the future of the United States and shared beliefs with Argentina’s right-wing leader.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Matias Baglietto/Reuters
item.media:description: Peter Thiel, right, arriving for a meeting with President Javier Milei at the Casa Rosada Presidential Palace in Buenos Aires in April.

item.title: Paul McCartney on His New Album and the Beatles’ Earliest Days
item.description: On his new album, the ex-Beatle revisits his past and revels in simply continuing to create.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Jim Dyson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Paul McCartney performing in London in 2024.

item.title: Happy, Bronx Zoo Elephant at Center of Animal Rights Case, Is Dead at 55
item.description: Activists had sued in a bid to secure her the fundamental human right to bodily liberty. Zoo officials said she was well cared for and called the lawsuit frivolous.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan
item.media:credit: Bebeto Matthews/Associated Press
item.media:description: Happy the elephant had lived at the Bronx Zoo since 1977.

item.title: What’s Inside New York’s $269 Billion Budget: Second-Home Tax, Restrictions on ICE and More
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul used the budget as a vehicle for policy initiatives, including a new second-home tax and a push to make the state less hospitable to ICE agents.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Kathy Hochul insisted on making climate goals easier to reach as part of the budget deal.

item.title: Mamdani Vowed to Back Espaillat. Now He May Endorse a D.S.A. Challenger.
item.description: The mayor is seriously considering getting behind Darializa Avila Chevalier, a democratic socialist challenging Representative Adriano Espaillat in an Upper Manhattan House primary.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg and Maya King
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times; Heather Diehl/Getty Images

item.title: A.I. Is Making Scams Hard to Spot. Here’s How to Protect Yourself.
item.description: A criminal could be masquerading as a celebrity, web store or family member asking for your money. Detecting scams requires a new approach.
item.dc:creator: Brian X. Chen
item.media:credit: Derek Abella

item.title: Lizzo Addresses the Backlash and Performs Live at The New York Times
item.description: During a special edition of “Popcast,” the singer discussed her new album and the public fallout from a series of lawsuits. Then she performed a stripped-down set.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli and Jon Caramanica
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: Three Ways Trump Is Losing the War in Iran
item.description: Three reasons Trump is losing the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: David French
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: What Cubans Really Think About Trump
item.description: A filmmaker asks residents of the embattled island what they would say to the U.S. president.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Seal

item.title: A Violent Education
item.description: We look at an extraordinary investigation into police officers in Texas schools.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times

item.title: Bare-Knuckle Tactics and Brash Promises: How Howard Lutnick Operates
item.description: Before becoming U.S. commerce secretary, Mr. Lutnick controlled 818 companies. A review of their dealings sheds light on his freewheeling first year in government.
item.dc:creator: Michael Rothfeld and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times

item.title: Genetic Scores are Booming. But Will Anti-Discrimination Laws Cover Your DNA?
item.description: As predictive medicine advances, legal scholars warn that decades-old federal guidelines could set up a potential clash between your genes and your job.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:credit: Biophoto Associates/Science Source
item.media:description: A scanning electron micrograph of human chromosomes. Polygenic risk scoring, increasingly popular in personal medicine, uses an individual’s genome to estimate that person’s likelihood of developing a specific disease.

item.title: Scientists Find a Potential Cure for Chronic Hepatitis B
item.description: Most people recover from the infection, but it poses great risks for those who don’t. A new drug may cure 1 in 5 of these patients.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Liz Hafalia/The San Francisco Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A hepatitis B vaccine being prepared in 2009.

item.title: The Real Story Behind “Pressure,” the Film About the D-Day Weather Forecast
item.description: The new film “Pressure” tells the story of the fateful D-Day weather forecast. Here’s what it got right and wrong from the historical record.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
